risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was in blocks of two stratified by infant gender." Comment: The paper reports block randomization with stratification by gender, which indicates a systematic random allocation process rather than a predictable non-random rule. Although the exact random-number method is not described, the reported randomization procedure is sufficient to judge low risk for random sequence generation.

Quote: "The allocation list was held by the pharmaceutical company and the Director of the Instituto de Investigacion Nutricional (IIN) until the end of the study." Comment: This indicates the person enrolling participants did not have access to the upcoming assignments; the allocation schedule was not available to recruiters, which is an explicit concealment mechanism preventing foreknowledge. The supplements were also pre-coded with each subject’s unique study identification number for distribution.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable." and "The allocation list was held by the pharmaceutical company and the Director of the Instituto de Investigacion Nutricional (IIN) until the end of the study." Comment: This indicates participants and study personnel distributing the supplements were effectively blinded to allocation. Given the outcomes were biochemical measures (hemoglobin, ferritin, copper, zinc), knowledge of assignment is unlikely to have influenced the outcome assessment or the intervention delivery in a way that would bias the dominant study outcomes.

Quote: "The bottles containing the supplements as well as the appearance of the supplements were indistinguishable." ... "Plasma ferritin was determined at the IIN laboratory by ELISA" and "Plasma copper and zinc and urinary zinc concentrations were determined at the IIN laboratory by atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS)"; "Hemoglobin and hematocrit were assessed at the clinic using standard methods by study nurse". Comment: Outcome assessment was mainly laboratory-based/standardized biochemical measurement, which is unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. The report does not explicitly state that the laboratory staff were blinded, but the outcomes are objective and measured by assay or standard methods rather than subjective judgment, so lack of assessor blinding would be unlikely to bias detection.

Quote: "As shown, there were 42 (13%) infants who left the study, leaving 108 in the iron + copper group and 101 in the iron + copper + zinc group." Comment: Overall attrition was modest (13%) and well below the usual concern threshold; the paper also states that "There were no differences in child, family characteristics, or initial hemoglobin concentrations" among those who completed versus those lost to follow up, suggesting no strong evidence that missingness was outcome-related. Although loss to follow-up was somewhat higher in the zinc group and some dropouts were due to dislike of the supplement or lack of time, the extent of attrition is still reasonable and the report provides enough information to judge the risk of bias as unlikely to materially affect the results.
